task_yaml.task:
End of the year and leadership wants a newsletter post about our sales
performance — something readable, not a data dump. The raw data lives
at /tmp_workspace/clawbench/sources/monthly_sales_2010.csv. Use the
CSV pipeline skill to load and aggregate the numbers, the Python visualization skill
for the cover chart, and the blog-writing skill to draft the
post so it actually reads like our newsletter voice.

The angle I want is a real year-in-review feel. The post should leave
readers with a sense of how the year unfolded month by month and the
MoM trend, which stores carried the load across the regional and store
ranking, when the peak hit and how concentrated that peak month was in
any single store, what changed in basket size versus order count over
the year using an average-order-value lens against order count, and
what we should watch for in the next quarter as a forward-looking
takeaway. Don't over-structure it with one header per topic — just let
those threads come through naturally in the writeup.

Give me three deliverables under /tmp_workspace/results/.

First, post.md at roughly 800 words, with an intro, three concrete
business callouts grounded in the CSV that stand on their own numbers
inside the body, and a short conclusion or recommendation at the end.

Second, cover.png — a combined bar-and-line chart I can drop in as the
post's cover image, with the monthly axis spanning the full year
present in the CSV.

Third, analysis.xlsx with three sheets. Monthly should have month,
total revenue, total orders, average order value, and month-over-month
revenue change driven by formulas. Store_Summary should have one row
per store or country with revenue, orders, share of total revenue, and
peak month, where the share-of-total and peak-month columns are
formula-backed so I can adjust the workbook later without rebuilding
it. Callouts should hold the three exact data points used in the blog
post, each with a cell reference back to whichever calculation sheet
produced it.

When you cite numbers in the post, use the same all-store totals that
drive the workbook so the post and the spreadsheet agree on every
figure.
